title: NOC 83101 – Oil and gas well drillers, servicers, testers and related workers | GTR Immigration
description: Professionals under NOC 83101 are the technical experts who drive Canada's world-class energy sector. As a TEER 3 occupation, these workers operate the high-precision drilling and hoisting machinery required for the exploration and production of oil and gas. Whether working on massive offshore rigs or land-based service units, they are responsible for rig setup, well completion, and complex testing procedures. In 2026, as Canada integrates "Clean-Tech" and carbon-sequestration drilling into traditional energy projects, these skilled tradespeople are more essential than ever. For immigration, this role is a primary candidate for the Federal Skilled Trades Program (FSTP) and the new 2026 TR to PR Pathway.

classification.teer: Classification LevelDetailsBroad Occupation Category8 – Natural resources, agriculture and related production occupationsTEER Level3Major Group83 – Occupations in natural resources and related productionSub-Major Group831 – Occupations in natural resources and related productionMinor Group8310 – Occupations in natural resources and related productionNOC Code83101

duties: Drillers & Servicers: Direct rig crews in setting up rigs and drilling, completing, or servicing oil and gas exploration wells. Operate controls of drilling and hoisting machinery.
Testers & Loggers: Drive specialized wireline or service trucks to well sites. Assemble and attach equipment/recorders to drill stems or wirelines to conduct tests.
Data Collection: Operate recorders and computers in mobile testing units to collect and interpret subsurface data.
Safety & Training: Ensure safety procedures are strictly followed on-site and train or arrange training for rig crew members.
Operations: Maintain detailed records of all drilling and servicing operations for regulatory and company reporting.
employment_requirements: Completion of secondary school is usually required.
Work Experience: Drillers and servicers typically require 4+ years of experience in subordinate rig crew positions (e.g., as a derrickman or motorhand).
Education: A college diploma in drilling or petroleum technology is often required for senior or offshore roles.
Certifications: Mandatory certificates include Blowout Prevention (BOP), H2S Awareness, First Aid, and WHMIS/TDG.
Trade Certification: Rig Technician certification is compulsory in Alberta and available (voluntary) in most other provinces. Red Seal endorsement is available for qualified technicians.
Physical Stamina: The ability to work in remote, high-pressure environments and perform physically demanding tasks is essential.
salary_prospects: Note: Total annual compensation for experienced drillers in 2026 often ranges between $118,000 and $155,000, depending on overtime and remote premiums.
job_outlook: The outlook is Favourable. Industry data suggests that while the energy sector is evolving toward "Net-Zero," the demand for skilled drillers to manage carbon-capture wells and traditional natural gas production remains high. Over 1,000 job openings are projected in B.C. and Alberta alone over the next decade, primarily driven by the expansion of the "Blue Economy" and the retirement of the current workforce.
labour_market_demand: The demand for oil and gas well drillers and testers is Very Good, particularly in Western Canada and the Atlantic offshore regions. In 2026, Alberta (Grand Prairie/Fort McMurray), Saskatchewan, and British Columbia (Northeast) are facing significant hiring gaps. With the rise of natural gas exports and new sub-surface exploration technologies, Canadian energy companies are actively seeking international talent with robust field experience to maintain production targets.
Global Energy Leader: Work with some of the world's most advanced drilling technology in a country recognized for its strict safety and environmental regulations.
Technological Integration: Gain experience with automated "Walking Rigs," directional drilling software, and real-time subsurface data analytics.
High Compensation: This is one of the highest-paying skilled trades in Canada, often featuring specialized "Camp Job" benefits and performance bonuses.
Strategic PR Pathways: This occupation is a high priority for Express Entry Category-Based Draws for "Trade Occupations" and the Alberta Opportunity Stream.
pathways: Federal Skilled Trades Program (FSTP): A high-priority route for experienced drillers with a job offer or provincial certification.
New 2026 TR to PR Pathway: A temporary measure targeting 33,000 workers already in Canada, with priority for "Skilled Trades" in energy and resource sectors.
Express Entry (Category-Based Selection): Targeted specifically for candidates in the "Trade Occupations" category.
Alberta Opportunity Stream: A fast-track PR route for those already working in Alberta’s oil and gas sector.
